scenario_title: tracking fish population in a coral reef
scenario_description: Marine Biology: tracking fish population in a coral reef

turns.text: I just took this photo of a coral reef during my dive. It looks really healthy with a lot of fish.
turns.image_description: Underwater photo of a coral reef with vibrant fish populations, showing 12 distinct species including parrotfish and angelfish, with no visible signs of bleaching.

turns.text: This shows a strong baseline biodiversity. I count 12 species with no bleaching—this reef is in excellent condition currently.
Here's the same area three months later. The fish numbers seem different now.
turns.image_description: Same reef 3 months later showing reduced numbers of parrotfish (now 5 individuals) but increased angelfish (now 15), with some coral bleaching visible.

turns.text:
What is the final ecological state of the reef based on the observed changes in fish populations and coral health?

B) 80% fish population loss with 70% coral bleaching
A) 12 species with no bleaching
C) 20% biomass loss and 30% bleaching
D) 50% algal coverage with stable fish populations

ground_truth.reasoning_chain: Turn 1 established baseline biodiversity. Turn 2 showed initial imbalance (parrotfish decline, angelfish increase) and early bleaching. Turn 3 quantified biomass loss and bleaching spread. Turn 4 revealed extreme population collapse but raised questions about permanence. Turn 5 linked losses to habitat degradation (structural damage, algal bloom). Turn 6 confirmed irreversible collapse with 80% fish loss and 70% bleaching.
ground_truth.key_difficulty: The full ecosystem collapse requires observing sequential population declines and cumulative habitat degradation

_meta.scenario: Marine Biology: tracking fish population in a coral reef
